draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: For a runner, a persistent sidebar showing current temperature, wind, and alerts is faster than hunting through tabs or cards
facade.label: Sidebar with live conditions
facade.acceptImplies: User values quick-glance weather data and favors persistent UI over modal/tab switching
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers focused primary view and relegates secondary conditions to secondary UI; likely wants minimal persistent chrome

evidence.hypothesis: For a runner, a persistent sidebar showing current temperature, wind, and alerts is faster than hunting through tabs or cards
evidence.implication: User values quick-glance weather data and favors persistent UI over modal/tab switching

facade.hypothesis: Weather app for runners benefits from high contrast, clean typography, and card-based layouts that prioritize readability at a glance while in motion
facade.axisTargeted: Light vs Dark / Complexity vs Simplicity
facade.label: Bright, minimal cards
facade.acceptImplies: User favors legible, spacious interfaces with strong visual hierarchy—likely prefers bold type, generous padding, minimal decoration
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer darker themes, denser information density, or more atmospheric/immersive visual styles that feel less clinical

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer clean, high-contrast displays that are easy to read in sunlight and don't feel cluttered during a run
facade.axisTargeted: Digital vs Organic — testing whether the app leans toward streamlined, technological clarity or natural, intuitive simplicity
facade.label: Bright, minimal interface
facade.acceptImplies: Users value speed and data legibility over warmth; interface should prioritize readability and task-focused design
facade.rejectImplies: Users may prefer a more grounded, less tech-forward aesthetic; consider softer typography, warmer color palettes, or more contextual visual breathing room

facade.hypothesis: Runners expect weather info to appear through continuous gesture rather than tap-and-wait, reducing cognitive load during active sessions
facade.axisTargeted: Fluid gesture-driven transitions vs discrete tap-based interaction
facade.label: Smooth swipes reveal data
facade.acceptImplies: Prioritize swipe/scroll as primary navigation; animate weather layers in with momentum; treat taps as secondary
facade.rejectImplies: Tap-and-load model is acceptable; instant state changes are preferred; gestures are nice-to-have, not core

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond better to encouraging, conversational tone than neutral weather reporting
facade.label: Upbeat coach voice
facade.acceptImplies: Design copy with motivational language, celebratory alerts, and direct address ('You're good to run!')
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward neutral, data-focused tone focused on facts and metrics without personality

facade.hypothesis: Runners want the current condition that matters most (temp, wind, humidity) displayed huge and immediate, not buried in a data table or competing with five other metrics
facade.axisTargeted: information density vs. scanability — whether to lead with a single dominant data point or show all metrics equally
facade.label: One big number
facade.acceptImplies: Prioritize hero metrics (e.g., wind speed, temperature) at large scale; de-emphasize secondary data; fast decision-making is the user need
facade.rejectImplies: Distribute metrics evenly across the interface; let users scan and compare multiple data points at once; density and completeness matter more than visual hierarchy

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets on home screen vs. navigating into detailed weather views
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather info
Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets on home screen vs. navigating into detailed weather views
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather info
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: For runners, metric-heavy cards (pace, heart rate, weather) arranged in a grid layout lets them scan multiple data points simultaneously without scrolling, versus a linear list that forces sequential reading
facade.label: Cards with live metrics
facade.acceptImplies: User prioritizes parallel data intake and visual comparison over narrative flow; favors glanceable layouts
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers sequential information hierarchy and simplicity; finds dense layouts overwhelming during activity

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
